---
title: "The Shape of Production"
---

Now we bring the earlier chapters together and look at the production recommendation.

## Why Production Changes the Choice

In a real platform, the requirements are broader than "the cluster came up once."

Production asks for:

- repeatable scaling
- safer upgrades
- visibility into status and degraded conditions
- consistent placement policy
- storage that survives rescheduling
- a cleaner interface for GitOps-style operations

That naturally pushes the recommendation toward an operator-centered model.

## The Platform Around the Cluster

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["GitOps or Automation"] --> B["CAPI-managed Kubernetes cluster"]
  B --> C["Operator"]
  C --> D["Valkey cluster resources"]
  D --> E["Pods across worker nodes and failure domains"]
  E --> F["CSI-backed persistent volumes"]
  A --> G["Load balancer entry path"]
  G --> H["API or client traffic"]
```

This is where infrastructure details start to matter: CAPI, portable CSI storage, load balancers, and placement across failure domains all influence whether the cluster remains resilient when the environment itself changes.

## What Must Be True for the Design to Hold

At a minimum, the design should preserve:

- stable cluster membership after restart when storage is retained
- shard-aware placement for primaries and replicas
- enough worker capacity to avoid collapsing multiple fault domains onto one node
- a clear status surface when scheduling, storage, or bootstrap steps fail

## The Production Verdict

The strongest direction is:

- use the no-operator path as a learning and debugging lens
- keep the scratch-built operator as proof of design understanding and as a place to test control ideas
- prefer an operator-first production model
- push the official or otherwise mature operator path toward the PVC and shard-affinity behavior the system really needs

In other words, the long-term win is not "start from zero forever." It is to carry our understanding into a durable operator path that respects how Valkey actually behaves.

## Summary

The cluster survives not because one component is clever, but because several layers agree on the same identity story:

- Kubernetes keeps resources moving
- the operator keeps intent and sequencing aligned
- Valkey keeps cluster truth through gossip and slot ownership
- networking keeps the right nodes reachable
- secrets keep trust intact
- persistent storage keeps memory attached to the returning member

That shared agreement is what makes recovery feel reliable instead of accidental.
